title: Exiting *insert mode*
subtitle: Three ways out, and why people remap.
summary: Esc, Ctrl-[, and Ctrl-C all exit *insert mode* — but they're not identical. Esc and Ctrl-[ trigger InsertLeave; Ctrl-C bypasses it.

blocks.text: There are three ways out of *insert mode*. Two are equivalent, one is subtly different — and that subtle difference matters.

blocks.title: Why Ctrl-[ Exists
blocks.text: On old terminals, {key:Esc} sent the byte `0x1b` — same byte produced by {key:Ctrl-[}. Vim never had to distinguish them; both still trigger the same exit logic. The {key:Ctrl-[} alias survives because (a) {key:Esc} is far away on modern keyboards, and (b) {key:Ctrl-[} is more comfortable for fast typists with Caps Lock remapped to Ctrl.

blocks.title: Ctrl-C Is NOT the Same
blocks.text: {key:Ctrl-C} exits *insert mode* but **skips the `InsertLeave` autocommand**. Plugins that auto-format, save, or update status on Insert-leave will not fire. It also doesn't abbreviate: typed `:abbreviate` expansions don't trigger. For everyday text editing, prefer {key:Esc} or {key:Ctrl-[}.
Many users remap {key:Caps Lock} → {key:Ctrl} at the OS level, then use {key:Ctrl-[} as their exit. It's the single biggest ergonomic win for Vim.

blocks.title: Insert exit = state flip + cursor clamp + undo boundary
blocks.text: Three things happen on Escape from *insert mode*, in order: the input transcript is finalized into the dot-repeat slot, an undo boundary is inserted, and the cursor is clamped back from the virtual one-past-end column to a real character. {key:Ctrl-C} skips the dot-repeat finalize step (which is why it doesn't update {key:.} the same way Esc does); {key:Ctrl-[} is a literal alias for Esc.
